title: Multiply fractions and whole numbers word problems
description: Practice solving multiplication word problems that include whole numbers and fractions.
topic_path: Module 5: Fraction equivalence, ordering, and operations
Topic G: Repeated addition of fractions as multiplication
Multiply fractions and whole numbers word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
You are planning a movie marathon.  Each movie lasts $\dfrac32$ hours.  You want to watch $4$ movies nonstop. 

**How many hours will your movie marathon last?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]]  hours
hints:
Each movie lasts $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ hours.


[[☃ image 1]]

You want to watch $\blueD{4}$ movies nonstop.


[[☃ image 1]]

You can multiply $\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ to figure out how long your movie marathon will last.

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD3} {\maroonD2}\\\\
&={\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$
Your movie marathon will last $6$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Katie's hair grows $\dfrac78$ of a centimeter each month.  

**How many centimeters will Katie's hair grow over $5$ months?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\text{cm}$
hints:
Katie's hair grows $\maroonD{\dfrac78}$ of a centimeter each month. We want to know how much her hair will grow over $\blueD5$ months. 


Month $\blueD1$ | Month $\blueD2$  | Month $\blueD3$ |Month $\blueD4$ | Month $\blueD5$
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Five equal-sized rectangles, each divided into eight equal parts with seven parts shaded pink. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled seven-eighths. Above the rectangles, each rectangle is numbered Month 1 to Month 5..](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cbfc20ad2aa5a332b54f657053aa71f37fa9611)|![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cbfc20ad2aa5a332b54f657053aa71f37fa9611)|![ ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cbfc20ad2aa5a332b54f657053aa71f37fa9611)|![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cbfc20ad2aa5a332b54f657053aa71f37fa9611)|![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cbfc20ad2aa5a332b54f657053aa71f37fa9611)

We can multiply $\blueD{5 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac78}$ to figure this out.

Katie's hair will grow $\dfrac{35}8 \text{cm}$.

This can also be written as $4\dfrac38 \text{cm}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Darren practiced piano $5$ days this week. He practiced $\dfrac34$ of an hour each day.  

**How many hours did Darren practice piano this week?**   

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  hours
hints:
Darren practiced $\maroonD{\dfrac34}$ of an hour each day.  He practiced $\blueD5$ days.


Day $\blueD1$ | Day $\blueD2$  | Day $\blueD3$  | Day $\blueD4$ | Day $\blueD5$ 
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Five equal-sized circles, each divided into four equal parts with three parts shaded pink. Each circle is labeled three-fourths.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20f5fd402b4bef283b1389be59f1f65a90da5eb1) |![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20f5fd402b4bef283b1389be59f1f65a90da5eb1) |![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20f5fd402b4bef283b1389be59f1f65a90da5eb1) |![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20f5fd402b4bef283b1389be59f1f65a90da5eb1) |![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20f5fd402b4bef283b1389be59f1f65a90da5eb1) 

We can multiply $\blueD{5 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac34}$ to figure out how many hours Darren practiced this week.

Darren practiced piano $\dfrac{15}{4}$ hours this week.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac34$ hours.

Talia attended a $5$-day math camp that lasted $\dfrac56$ of an hour each day.

Above the circles, the circles are numbered Day 1 to Day 5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2baf1d4c23cbd6f4fc52e498049d62a504794795)| ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2baf1d4c23cbd6f4fc52e498049d62a504794795) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2baf1d4c23cbd6f4fc52e498049d62a504794795)|![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2baf1d4c23cbd6f4fc52e498049d62a504794795)|![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2baf1d4c23cbd6f4fc52e498049d62a504794795)

We can multiply $\blueD5 \times\maroonD{\dfrac56}$ to figure out Talia's total hours in math camp.

Talia spent $\dfrac{25}6$ hours at math camp.

assessment_items.question_content:
A batch of brownies requires $\dfrac13$ of a can of cocoa powder.  You want to make $4$ batches.

**How many cans of cocoa powder do you need?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cans
hints:
Each batch requires $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$ of a can of cocoa powder.  You want to make $\blueD4$ batches.

Batch $\blueD1$ | Batch $\blueD2$  | Batch $\blueD3$ |Batch $\blueD4$ 
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Four equal-sized rectangles, each divided into three equal parts with one part shaded pink. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled one-third. Above the rectangles, each rectangle is numbered Batch 1 to Batch 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41)

You can multiply $\blueD4 \times\maroonD{\dfrac13}$ to figure out how many cans of cocoa powder you need.

You need $\dfrac{4}3$ cans of cocoa powder.

This can also be written as $1\dfrac13$ cans.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aliana uses beads that are $\dfrac54$ centimeters long to make a necklace. She uses $3$ beads all together.

**How many centimeters of beads will Aliana use for the necklace?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints:
Each bead has a length of $\maroonD{\dfrac54}$ centimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

Aliana uses  $\blueD{3}$ beads.



[[☃ image 1]]



We can multiply $\blueD{3 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac54}$ to figure out the total length of the beads.
${\blueD3 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac54}=\dfrac{{\blueD3 \times }\:\maroonD5} {\maroonD4}={\dfrac{15}{4}}$

Aliana will use $\dfrac{15}4$ centimeters of beads for the necklace.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac34$ centimeters.

You need $\dfrac{9}4$ jars  of peanut butter.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ameer uses $\dfrac14$ of a bottle of shampoo to give each of his dogs a bath.  He has $5$ dogs all together.

**How many bottles of shampoo does Ameer need?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] bottles
hints:
Ameer uses $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ of a bottle of shampoo on each dog.  He has $\blueD5$ dogs.

Dog $\blueD1$ | Dog $\blueD2$  | Dog $\blueD3$ | Dog $\blueD4$ | Dog $\blueD5$ 
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-:  |:-: |:-: 
![Five equal-sized rectangles, each divided into four equal parts with one part shaded pink. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled one-fourth. Above the rectangles, each rectangle is numbered Dog 1 to Dog 5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf08068e6b91bd8ae1024c79e468ad57cdd58bb2)| ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf08068e6b91bd8ae1024c79e468ad57cdd58bb2) |![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf08068e6b91bd8ae1024c79e468ad57cdd58bb2) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf08068e6b91bd8ae1024c79e468ad57cdd58bb2) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf08068e6b91bd8ae1024c79e468ad57cdd58bb2)

You can multiply $\blueD5 \times\maroonD{\dfrac14}$ to figure out how many bottles of shampoo Ameer needs.
${\blueD5 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac14}=\dfrac{{\blueD5 \times }\:\maroonD1} {\maroonD4}={\dfrac{5}{4}}$

Ameer needs $\dfrac{5}4$ bottles of shampoo.

This can also be written as $1\dfrac14$ bottles.

assessment_items.question_content:
An art teacher buys clay for an animation project. Each box of clay weighs $\dfrac52$ kilograms.  She buys $2$ boxes of clay.

**How many kilograms of clay did the teacher buy?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilograms
hints:
Each box of clay weighs $\maroonD{\dfrac52}$ kilograms. The teacher buys $\blueD2$ boxes.

Box $\blueD1$ | Box $\blueD2$  
:-: |:-: 
![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with both parts shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of two-halves. Above the rectangle is the label Box 1 with 1 written in blue.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d650561c2c545494b2aeadff2c0ca78fd6ac0d3a)![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with both parts shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of two-halves. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d650561c2c545494b2aeadff2c0ca78fd6ac0d3a)![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with one part shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of one-half.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eeac579972bfb9522493ceb14d4b98ff68a41b08)|![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with both parts shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of two-halves. Above the rectangle is the label Box 2 with 2 written in blue.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d650561c2c545494b2aeadff2c0ca78fd6ac0d3a)![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with both parts shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of two-halves.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d650561c2c545494b2aeadff2c0ca78fd6ac0d3a)![A rectangle divided into two equal parts with one part shaded pink. Under the rectangle is a label of one-half.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eeac579972bfb9522493ceb14d4b98ff68a41b08)

You can multiply $\blueD2 \times\maroonD{\dfrac52}$ to figure out how many kilograms of clay the teacher buys.
${\blueD2 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac52}=\dfrac{{\blueD2 \times }\:\maroonD5} {\maroonD2}={\dfrac{10}{2}}$

The teacher buys $\dfrac{10}2$ kilograms of clay.

This can also be written as $5$ kilograms.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sandy bought $6$ medium cartons of blueberries. Each medium carton held $\dfrac3{4}$ liter of blueberries. 

Lindsay bought $2$ large cartons of blueberries. Each large carton held $\dfrac9{4}$ liters of blueberries. 

**Who bought more liters of blueberries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Sandy

Sandy bought $6$ cartons with $\dfrac34$ liter of blueberries in each carton.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{6 \times \dfrac34}$ to figure out how many liters of blueberries Sandy bought:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6 \times \dfrac34}\\\\
=&\dfrac{\blueD{6 \times}\blueD3} {\blueD4}\\\\
=&\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}
\end{align}$

Sandy bought $\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters of blueberries.

###Lindsay

Lindsay bought $2$ cartons with $\dfrac94$ liter of blueberries in each carton.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac94}$ to figure out how many liters of blueberries Lindsay bought:

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac94}\\\\
=&\dfrac{\maroonD{2 \times}\maroonD9} {\maroonD4}\\\\
=&\maroonD{\dfrac{18}{4}}
\end{align}$

Sandy |  | Lindsay
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters | $=$ | $\maroonD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters
They bought the same number of liters of blueberries.

choices.content: They bought the same number of liters of blueberries.

assessment_items.question_content:
Last week, Becky went to dance class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ hours.

This week, Becky went to dance class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ hour.

**Which week did Becky spend more total time in dance class?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Last week

Last week, Becky went to class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class last week: 

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\blueD 3} {\blueD2}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=\blueD{6}
\end{align}$

Becky went to class for $\blueD{6}$ hours last week.

###This week

This week, Becky went to class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{5 \times 1}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class this week: 

$\maroonD5\times\maroonD1=\maroonD5$

Becky went to class for $\maroonD{5}$ hours this week.

Last week |  | This week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{6}$ hours | $>$ | $\maroonD{5}$ hours
Becky spent more total time in dance class last week.

choices.content: She spent the same amount of time in dance class each week.

assessment_items.question_content:
Raul bought $6$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac23$ of them.

Omar bought $8$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac14$ of them.

**Who ate more tangerines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Eating a fraction of the tangerines is the same as eating that fraction times the number of tangerines each person had.

###Raul

Raul ate $\blueD{\dfrac23 \text{ of }6}$ tangerines.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{\dfrac23\times6}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Raul ate: 

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac23\text{ of }6} &=~ \blueD{\dfrac23 \times 6}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{2\times6}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{12}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD4
\end{align}$

Raul ate $\blueD4$ tangerines.

###Omar 

Omar ate $\maroonD{\dfrac14 \text{ of }8}$ tangerines.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{\dfrac14\times8}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Omar ate:

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\dfrac14\text{ of }8} ~&=~ \maroonD{\dfrac14 \times 8}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{1\times8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD2
\end{align}$

Omar ate $\maroonD2$ tangerines.

Raul |  | Omar
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD4$ tangerines | $>$ | $\maroonD2$ tangerines
Raul ate more tangerines.

choices.content: They ate the same number of tangerines.

assessment_items.question_content:
Liz and Erin are different ages but eat from the same size of bowl.

Liz ate $4 $ servings of cereal. Each serving was $\dfrac13 $ of a bowl.

Erin ate $3$ servings of cereal. Each serving was $\dfrac23 $ of a bowl.

**Who ate more bowls of cereal?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Liz

Serving $\maroonD1$ | Serving $\maroonD2$  | Serving $\maroonD3$ | Serving $\maroonD4$ 
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Four equal-sized rectangles, each divided into three equal parts with one part shaded pink. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled one-third. Above the rectangles, the rectangles are numbered Serving 1 to Serving 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41)

We can multiply $\maroonD{4 \times \dfrac13}$ to figure out how many bowls of cereal Liz ate:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{4 \times \dfrac13}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{4 \times}\maroonD1} {\maroonD3}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}
\end{align}$

Liz ate $\maroonD{\dfrac43}$ bowls of cereal.

###Erin

Serving $\blueD1$ | Serving $\blueD2$  | Serving $\blueD3$
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Three equal-sized rectangles, each divided into three equal parts with two parts shaded blue. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled two-thirds. Above the rectangles, each rectangle is numbered Serving 1 to Serving 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea)

We can multiply $\blueD{3 \times \dfrac23}$ to figure out how many bowls of cereal Erin ate:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{3 \times \dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{3 \times}\blueD2} {\blueD3}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{6}{3}}
\end{align}$

Erin ate $\blueD{\dfrac63}$ bowls of cereal.

Liz |  | Erin
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ bowls | $<$ | $\blueD{\dfrac{6}{3}}$ bowls
Erin ate more bowls of cereal.

choices.content: They ate the same amount of cereal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

**Who ran more kilometers in total?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Tess

Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac43}$ to figure out the total distance Tess ran:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac43}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{2 \times}\maroonD4} {\maroonD3}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}}
\end{align}$

Tess ran $\maroonD{\dfrac83}$ kilometers.

###Cam

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}$ to figure out the total distance Cam ran:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{5 \times}\blueD2} {\blueD3}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}}
\end{align}$

Cam ran $\blueD{\dfrac{10}3}$ kilometers.

Tess |  | Cam
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}} \text{ km}$ | $<$ | $\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}} \text{ km}$
Cam ran more kilometers in total.

choices.content: They ran the same number of total kilometers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rishi spent $\dfrac34$ of an hour each day for $2$ days working on his science project.

Kim spent $\dfrac14$ of an hour each day for $6$ days working on his science project. 

**Who spent more time in total working on their science project?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Rishi

Rishi spent $\dfrac34$ of an hour each day for $2$ days working on his science project.

[[☃ image 1]]


We can multiply $\blueD{2 \times \dfrac34}$ to figure out how many hours Rishi spent working on his science project:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{2 \times \dfrac34}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{2 \times}\blueD3} {\blueD4}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{6}{4}}
\end{align}$

Rishi spent $\blueD{\dfrac64}$ hour working on his science project.

###Kim

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{6 \times \dfrac14}$ to figure out how many hours Kim spent working on his science project:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{6 \times \dfrac14}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{6 \times}\maroonD1} {\maroonD4}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{4}}
\end{align}$

Kim spent $\maroonD{\dfrac64}$ hour working on her science project.

Rishi |  | Kim
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{\dfrac{6}{4}}$ hours | $=$ | $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{4}}$ hours
They spent the same amount of total time working on their projects.

choices.content: They spent the same amount of total time working on their projects.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kai ran $\dfrac35$ of a kilometer yesterday.  Today, he ran $6$ times as far.  

**How many kilometers did Kai run today?**  


[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{km}$
hints:
Kai ran $\maroonD{\dfrac35}$ of a kilometer yesterday.



[[☃ image 1]]

He ran $\blueD{6}$ times as far today.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{6 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac35}$ to figure out how far Kai ran today.

Kai ran $\dfrac{18}5 \text{ km}$ today.

We can also write this as $3\dfrac35 \text{ km}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits on it as the tomato plant has. The tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it. 

**How many fruits does the pepper plant have on it?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fruits
hints:
A tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it.

![9 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25f5c8fbc09be9480b3c740ba974dae49fdfe9be)

The pepper plant has $\dfrac23$ as many fruits as the tomato plant.

Let's break the $9$ tomatoes into $3$ equal groups.

![The same 9 circles in 3 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3c6172f2db0c563ee435bfa4cab1f0b0cfb48b21)

The pepper plant has $2$ of the $3$ equal groups.

![6 circles in 2 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a7b84b16f38612f53c8a4b0a69b9d739ac0e58c5)

There are $6$ peppers.

Since the pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits, we can also multiply $9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}$ to find how many fruits are on each plant.

$\begin{align}
&9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{9 \times 2} {3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{18}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac61\\\\
=&{6}
\end{align}$
The pepper plant has $6$ fruits on it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sophie's dog has $8$ chew toys. Annie's dog has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ as many chew toys as Sophie's dog.

**How many chew toys does Annie's dog have?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] chew toys
hints:
Sophie's dog has $8$ chew toys.

![8 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/554b2f377f347babd2228e9e733f109283df82ce)

Annie's dog has $\dfrac14$ as many chew toys as Sophie's dog.

Let's break the $8$ chew toys into $4$ equal groups.

![The same 8 circles in 4 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e9ae586963c5d9533193905a306bcf45de9b58a2)

Annie's dog has $1$ of the $4$ equal groups.

![2 circles in 1 group of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d419f1ebfed90f4a0efed58c146fc19f941cb5f)

There are $2$ chew toys.

Since Annie's dog has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ as many chew toys, we can also multiply $8 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$ to find how many chew toys she has.

$\begin{align}
&8 \times \dfrac{1}{4}\\\\
=&\dfrac{8 \times 1} {4}\\\\
=&\dfrac{8}{4}\\\\
=&{2}
\end{align}$
Annie's dog has $2$ chew toys.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mark and Damien were in a hot dog eating contest. Mark ate $12$ hot dogs. Damien ate $\dfrac46$ as many hot dogs as Mark.

**How many hot dogs did Damien eat?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hot dogs
hints:
Mark ate $12$ hot dogs.


[[☃ image 1]]

Damien ate $\dfrac46$ as many hot dogs as Mark..

Let's break the $12$ hot dogs into $6$ equal groups.

![The same 12 hot dogs in 6 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)

Damien ate $4$ of the $6$ equal groups.

![8 hot dogs in 4 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)

There are $8$ hot dogs.

Since Damien ate $\dfrac{4}{6}$ as many hot dogs, we can also multiply $12 \times \dfrac{4}{6}$ to find how many hot dogs he ate.

$\begin{align}
&12 \times \dfrac{4}{6}\\\\
=&\dfrac{12 \times 4} {6}\\\\
=&\dfrac{48}{6}\\\\
=&{8}
\end{align}$
Damien ate $8$ hot dogs.

assessment_items.question_content:
A gardener digs a flower bed that is  $7$ meters long and $\dfrac{3}{4}$ meter wide. 

**What is the area of the flower bed?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square meters
hints:
The flower bed is $\blueD7$ meters long.


[[☃ image 1]]

The flower bed is $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ meter wide.



[[☃ image 1]]
The area of the flower bed is $\dfrac{21}4$ square meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dina is getting a new rug for her hallway. The rug is $\dfrac56$ meter wide and $9$ meters long.

**What is the area of the rug?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square meters
hints:
The rug is $\blueD9$ meters long.



[[☃ image 1]]

The rug is $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$ meter wide.


[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the rug is $\dfrac{45}6$ square meters.

We can also write $\dfrac{45}6$ as $\dfrac{15}2$ square meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chris is putting new baseboards in his bedroom. The baseboard is $15$ meters long and $\dfrac9{10}$ meter wide.

**What is the area of the baseboard?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square meters
hints:
The baseboard is $\blueD{15}$ meters long.

[[☃ image 1]]

The basebooard is $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{10}}$ meter wide.



[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the baseboard is $\dfrac{135}{10}$ square meters.

We can also write $\dfrac{135}{10}$ as $\dfrac{27}2$ square meters.
